fbsql: SQL is the user API (functions inside PostgreSQL)
spark_mllib: SQL (spark.sql) defines relations; the model is fit through the DataFrame/Pipeline API
notes: there is no SQL function like fit_glm()/predict_glm(); ML lives outside the SQL language

fbsql: fbsql.fit_glm(relation SQL string; R formula; family) returning a relation
notes: verified with Spark 3.5.1 local mode (official image + numpy)

fbsql: R formula string with R semantics
spark_mllib: RFormula(formula='churn_flag ~ age + gender') -- formula-shaped but NOT R semantics
notes: key finding: StringIndexer orders levels by FREQUENCY and drops the last category; here the reference level is 'Other' instead of R's first sorted level 'F'

fbsql: term-named rows matching R's glm() exactly
spark_mllib: same fitted model but a different parameterization (verified numerically: intercept_spark = intercept_fbsql + genderOther_fbsql etc.); term names like gender_F live in ML attribute metadata
notes: coefficient tables are not directly comparable across the two systems even though predictions are identical; hazardous when porting R workflows

fbsql: fbsql.predict_glm(relation; model; on_new_levels) returning the scored relation
spark_mllib: model.transform(dataframe) returning the input DataFrame plus prediction (and features/label) columns
notes: transform is closure-like at the DataFrame level -- but in the DataFrame world; not exposed to SQL

spark_mllib: 'prediction' column on the response scale (probability for binomial GLR)
notes: observed: c101 0.0406 / c102 0.9794 / c103 0.4280 -- identical to FbSQL/R at 4 decimals

spark_mllib: PipelineModel (JVM object graph; persisted as a directory via .save())

fbsql: metadata jsonb column inside the returned relation
spark_mllib: ML attribute metadata attached to the features column + fitted stage state (e.g. StringIndexer labels) inside the PipelineModel
notes: structured but object-bound; term names had to be extracted programmatically from column metadata

fbsql: Complete Case at fit; NULL predictors predict NULL
spark_mllib: default handleInvalid='error': transform FAILS on NULL features (observed); handleInvalid='skip' silently DROPS the rows (observed: 5 rows in -> 3 rows out)
notes: the skip policy changes the output granularity -- FbSQL keeps the row with a NULL prediction

spark_mllib: the same handleInvalid setting governs unseen labels: error by default; 'skip' drops the row (observed); 'keep' buckets unseen levels (documented; not exercised)
notes: Spark couples NULL handling and novel-level handling into one switch and offers no keep-the-row-as-NULL option

fbsql: yes (any SQL string defining a relation)
spark_mllib: yes at the DataFrame level (fit/transform take DataFrames including spark.sql results)

fbsql: yes (one relation from a set-returning function)
spark_mllib: transform returns a DataFrame (input columns + appended columns) -- closure at the DataFrame level; fit returns a model object; nothing is a SQL-level relation
host-language keyword arguments and setters (Python/Scala); not SQL named arguments
offsetCol / weightCol parameters exist on GeneralizedLinearRegression (documented; not exercised)
RFormula documents support for ':' and '*' operators (not exercised)

fbsql: deterministic; R parity locked by pg_regress
spark_mllib: GLR/IRLS is deterministic; stochastic algorithms expose a seed parameter
notes: environment pinned by docker/spark/Dockerfile (official image + numpy)
